heroOverview: A cecoureterocele is a rare congenital malformation in which the distal end of a ureter balloons into a cyst-like pouch that extends beneath the trigone of the bladder and protrudes into the urethra, yet remains open at its tip. Unlike an orthotopic ureterocele, the cecoureterocele's orifice lies within the bladder but its body prolipses past the bladder neck. Because this distal projection can intermittently obstruct the urethra, affected children — most of them girls with duplex collecting systems — may present at birth with voiding difficulty or recurrent urinary infections before the structural anomaly is identified on imaging. Cecoureterocele accounts for roughly 5-10% of all ureteroceles and is classified under ICD-10 code Q62.32 within the broad category of congenital obstructive defects of the urinary tract. Early recognition matters because progressive obstruction can silently damage the ipsilateral kidney before symptoms prompt investigation.

keyStats.prevalence: Ureteroceles overall occur in approximately 1 in 4,000 children; cecoureterocele represents an estimated 5-10% of that group
keyStats.demographics: Predominantly affects female infants and young girls (~80% of ureterocele cases); most identified before age 2
keyStats.avgAge: Most cases detected in infancy or early childhood, often prompted by antenatal hydronephrosis or febrile urinary infection
keyStats.globalCases: Rare; precise global incidence not established, but ureteroceles as a class are among the more common obstructive urinary anomalies of childhood

definition: A cecoureterocele (ICD-10: Q62.32) is a congenital cystic dilatation of the submucosal portion of the ureter as it enters the bladder, characterised by a wide-mouthed or open orifice positioned within the bladder trigone whose body extends as a tongue of tissue under the urothelium into and beyond the bladder neck toward the urethra. It is categorised as a subtype of ureterocele — a cystic ballooning of the intravesical ureter — and is distinguished from the orthotopic form, in which the pouch remains entirely within the bladder, and from the ectopic form, in which the ureteral orifice opens below the bladder neck. The word 'ceco' derives from Latin for 'blind pouch,' reflecting the characteristic tip morphology. Cecoureteroceles most commonly arise in the upper pole ureter of a completely duplicated (duplex) collecting system, though they can occur with a single system. The anomaly results from a developmental failure of the ureteral bud to remodel properly as it incorporates into the trigone, leaving an abnormal submucosal segment. The ipsilateral upper pole moiety is almost invariably obstructed, leading to variable degrees of hydronephrosis and dysplasia; the lower pole and contralateral kidney are usually normal. Vesicoureteral reflux into the ipsilateral lower pole — caused by distortion of the trigone — occurs in 50% or more of cases. Specialist management involves paediatric urology and may require input from nephrology when renal function is compromised.

typesClassification.description: The defining subtype: the ureterocele body extends beneath the trigone into the urethra but the orifice remains intravesical. It can produce intermittent urethral obstruction and prolapse.
The entire ureterocele pouch lies within the bladder trigone, with the orifice at or near its normal position. Less likely to cause urethral obstruction but can impair ipsilateral drainage.
The ureteral orifice opens below the bladder neck — in the urethra or beyond — causing continuous dribbling incontinence in girls because urine bypasses the sphincter.
Arises from a non-duplicated ureter; less common than duplex-associated forms and often orthotopic in girls, more frequently ectopic in boys.

primarySymptoms: Recurrent febrile urinary tract infections — the hallmark presentation in infancy, driven by pooled urine above the obstructed ureterocele acting as a culture medium.
Voiding difficulty or urinary retention — the cecoureterocele tongue prolapses into the urethra during voiding, partially blocking outflow, producing straining or interrupted stream.
Urinary incontinence or constant dribbling — paradoxical leakage occurs when the prolapsed pouch prevents complete closure of the urethral sphincter.
Antenatally detected hydronephrosis — many cases are now first suspected on routine second-trimester ultrasound showing upper pole dilatation.
Abdominal or flank mass — a markedly dilated upper pole moiety can be palpated as a smooth flank fullness in a neonate or young infant.
Failure to thrive or poor feeding — in neonates with severe bilateral obstruction or concurrent renal impairment, weight gain is delayed.
Visible prolapsing mass at the urethral meatus — rare but dramatic presentation in girls when the cecoureterocele prolapses completely through the urethra.
Haematuria — blood-stained urine occurs when infected or inflamed urothelium of the ureterocele wall bleeds.
Hypertension — less common, but chronic upper pole obstruction with secondary dysplasia can drive renin-mediated blood pressure elevation.
Poor urinary stream in boys — though rare, boys present with a weak stream if the ureterocele tongue blocks the posterior urethra during voiding.
earlyWarningSigns: Upper pole hydronephrosis detected on prenatal ultrasound after 20 weeks' gestation.
First febrile UTI in an infant under 12 months of age prompting imaging.
Straining or prolonged voiding time noted by parents or during newborn assessment.
Persistent low-grade pyuria on routine infant urinalysis.
emergencySigns: Complete urinary retention in a neonate or young infant — a urological emergency requiring immediate catheterisation.
Visible prolapse of a smooth glistening mass at the urethral meatus in a girl — the ureterocele has extruded and requires urgent reduction.
Signs of urosepsis: high fever, rigors, poor perfusion, or lethargy in an infant with known or suspected urinary anomaly.
Rapidly rising serum creatinine suggesting bilateral obstruction or loss of solitary functional kidney.
Septic shock in a child with a febrile UTI and an undiagnosed duplex anomaly — mandates immediate resuscitation and antibiotic therapy.

causes.description: The ureteral bud arises from the mesonephric duct around the 5th week of gestation. Failure of normal incorporation and remodelling of the terminal ureter into the trigone leaves an obstructed submucosal segment, which dilates into a cystic pouch under normal intraluminal urine pressure.

Even when the cecoureterocele orifice is wide, a functional mismatch between the peristaltic wave and the compliant pouch creates intermittent obstruction, trapping urine and promoting dilatation over time during fetal and postnatal life.

A duplicated renal pelvis produces two ureteral buds. The upper pole bud, migrating more caudally along the trigone, is the one prone to ureterocele formation. Abnormal bud positioning predisposes the upper-pole ureter to both obstruction and cecoureterocele morphology.

Normal resorption of the common excretory duct (Chwalla's membrane) depends on apoptotic and proliferative signals. Incomplete membrane dissolution leaves residual obstructing tissue at the ureteral tip, driving the characteristic pouch formation of cecoureterocele.

No single gene has been firmly implicated, but mutations in genes regulating GDNF-RET signalling and ureteral bud branching — including ROBO2 and GATA3 variants — have been found in kindreds with multiple ureterocele and duplex anomalies, suggesting a genetic predisposition.

riskFactors.description: Approximately 80% of ureterocele cases occur in girls, reflecting sex-linked differences in ureteral bud migration and trigonal remodelling during embryogenesis.
The vast majority of cecoureteroceles arise in the upper pole of a duplex system. Complete duplication carries a roughly 10-fold higher risk of ureterocele than a single system.

Duplex kidneys and ureteroceles cluster in families. First-degree relatives have an estimated 5-12% recurrence risk for urinary duplex anomalies, suggesting autosomal dominant inheritance with variable expressivity.
Ureteroceles are reported at higher rates in white children compared to African American or Asian children in multicentre US and European series, though ascertainment bias may contribute.

Congenital anomalies of the kidney and urinary tract (CAKUT) are associated with copy number variants and single-gene mutations; an infant with another CAKUT finding has heightened risk for associated ureterocele.
Some CAKUT registry data link first-trimester maternal hyperglycaemia to increased urinary tract malformation rates, though the specific contribution to ureterocele risk is not quantified.

affectedDemographics: Female infants under 2 years — the dominant affected group
Children with prenatally detected hydronephrosis referred for postnatal imaging
Infants presenting with a first febrile UTI, triggering diagnostic ultrasound
Families with a prior child or sibling with a duplex kidney anomaly
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis of cecoureterocele relies on imaging and is often initiated after antenatal ultrasound shows upper pole hydronephrosis, after a first febrile UTI in an infant, or following the discovery of a prolapsed urethral mass. Postnatal renal-bladder ultrasound is the first-line study and may show a cystic intravesical structure at the bladder base, dilatation of the upper pole pelvis and ureter, and a dilated ureter tracking behind the bladder. However, ultrasound alone cannot reliably distinguish cecoureterocele from orthotopic or ectopic ureterocele and may underestimate the full extent of the subvesical tongue, especially in dehydrated neonates where a collapsed pouch may not be visible. Voiding cystourethrogram (VCUG) is essential: it delineates the intravesical and intraurethral extent of the pouch, identifies vesicoureteral reflux into the ipsilateral lower pole or contralateral ureter (present in 50% or more), and demonstrates the characteristic filling defect that expands under bladder filling and changes with voiding. Nuclear renal scintigraphy with DMSA or MAG3 quantifies differential function of the upper and lower pole moieties — a key factor in deciding whether the obstructed upper pole warrants salvage or resection. MR urography is increasingly used in specialist centres to provide anatomical detail of the duplex anatomy, the ureterocele morphology, and the renal parenchyma without radiation, though it requires sedation in young children. Cystoscopy under anaesthesia confirms the diagnosis and characterises the orifice location, and is often performed immediately before endoscopic treatment.

diagnosticTests.purpose: First-line study to identify upper pole hydronephrosis, the cystic intravesical pouch, and the dilated upper pole ureter behind the bladder.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A painless scan with a gel-covered probe on the abdomen; takes 20-30 minutes; the bladder should be full for optimal views of the ureterocele.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Defines the intravesical and urethral extent of the ureterocele, grades any vesicoureteral reflux, and guides surgical planning.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A urinary catheter is inserted; contrast fills the bladder under fluoroscopy; the child is asked to void so the urethral phase is captured. Mild discomfort from catheterisation; the procedure takes 30-45 minutes.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies split renal function and identifies parenchymal scarring from prior infection or dysplasia in the upper pole moiety.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: An intravenous injection of a small radiotracer; images taken at 2-4 hours; no sedation required in most children; low radiation dose.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Assesses drainage dynamics and degree of obstruction, especially if the upper pole function is borderline and a salvage procedure is being considered.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Similar to DMSA; includes a diuretic injection partway through the scan; imaging takes about 45 minutes.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Provides detailed anatomical delineation of duplex morphology, ureterocele extent, and renal parenchymal volume without ionising radiation, useful when ultrasound and VCUG leave diagnostic uncertainty.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Requires general anaesthesia or deep sedation in young children; imaging takes 45-60 minutes; no radiation.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Direct visualisation of the ureterocele orifice position and morphology; confirms whether the orifice is intravesical (cecoureterocele) and is performed before or during endoscopic incision.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Performed under general anaesthesia; a small camera is passed through the urethra; usually combined with the therapeutic procedure in the same session.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of cecoureterocele is surgical and is individualised based on ipsilateral upper pole function, the degree of obstruction and reflux, the child's age and general health, and the centre's experience. The overarching goals are to relieve obstruction, eliminate or reduce vesicoureteral reflux, protect overall renal function, and prevent recurrent urinary tract infections. For most children, management follows a staged or definitive strategy. Endoscopic puncture (incision) of the ureterocele is the least invasive first step: under cystoscopy, a holmium laser or electrocautery hook makes a small incision at the base of the ureterocele, decompressing the pouch and unroofing the obstruction. It is an effective temporising measure and can be definitive in up to 30-40% of single-system cases, but cecoureterocele often produces new reflux after puncture, and additional surgery is commonly required. Open or laparoscopic upper pole heminephroureterectomy — removal of the non-functional dysplastic upper pole moiety and its ureter — is the definitive treatment when upper pole function is less than 10-15% of the total kidney function (the situation in the majority of duplex cecoureteroceles). Lower urinary tract reconstruction, including ureterocele excision and ureteric re-implantation, is reserved for cases where the ipsilateral lower pole has reflux that has not resolved, where the contralateral ureter also refluxes, or where the bladder trigone has been significantly distorted. Antibiotic prophylaxis is maintained throughout the diagnostic and surgical period to prevent febrile UTI and upper tract damage. Long-term follow-up with imaging and blood pressure monitoring is recommended because chronic obstruction and repeated infections can cause progressive nephropathy.

medicalTreatments.name: Antibiotic prophylaxis
medicalTreatments.description: Low-dose daily trimethoprim or cephalexin is given from diagnosis until definitive surgical correction to prevent febrile UTIs and upper tract scarring. Initiated in all infants diagnosed postnatally, including those with prenatally detected hydronephrosis.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces febrile UTI frequency by approximately 50-60% during the pre-operative waiting period based on CAKUT prophylaxis trial data.

medicalTreatments.name: Endoscopic ureterocele incision (puncture)
medicalTreatments.description: Cystoscopic incision of the ureterocele base with holmium laser or electrocautery relieves obstruction immediately. Preferred initial procedure in ill neonates with urosepsis or bilateral obstruction and in single-system ureteroceles.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Decompresses obstruction in over 90% of cases; achieves long-term cure without further surgery in approximately 30-40% of single-system and up to 15-25% of duplex cases.

medicalTreatments.name: Upper pole heminephroureterectomy
medicalTreatments.description: Laparoscopic or open removal of the dysplastic upper pole renal moiety and as much of the upper pole ureter as possible. The procedure of choice when upper pole differential function is below 10-15%.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Resolves ipsilateral upper pole obstruction in essentially 100% of cases; subsequent reflux resolution occurs in approximately 60-75% of cases without lower tract reconstruction.

medicalTreatments.name: Ureterocele excision with ureteric re-implantation
medicalTreatments.description: Open reconstruction that excises the ureterocele and reimplants the ureters into the bladder using an anti-reflux technique. Used when reflux or trigonal distortion persists after upper tract surgery or is severe at presentation.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Achieves reflux resolution in approximately 90-95% of children and near-complete elimination of recurrent febrile UTI in long-term series.

medicalTreatments.name: Nephroureterectomy for non-functional kidney
medicalTreatments.description: When the ipsilateral kidney — rather than just the upper pole — is globally dysplastic or non-functional, complete nephroureterectomy is preferred to heminephroureterectomy.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Definitive; recurrent UTI attributable to the removed unit resolves in virtually 100% of cases.

surgicalOptions.name: Endoscopic ureterocele incision
surgicalOptions.description: Cystoscopic puncture or laser incision of the ureterocele base; minimally invasive, performed as a day case or short inpatient stay.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Decompression success >90%; curative (no further surgery) in ~30-40% of single-system and ~15-25% of duplex cases

surgicalOptions.name: Laparoscopic upper pole heminephroureterectomy
surgicalOptions.description: Retroperitoneoscopic or transperitoneal removal of the upper pole moiety; the preferred approach in experienced paediatric urology centres for duplex cecoureterocele with minimal upper pole function.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Near 100% resolution of upper pole obstruction; reflux resolution in approximately 60-75% without lower tract reconstruction

surgicalOptions.name: Open ureterocele excision and ureteric reimplantation
surgicalOptions.description: Lower urinary tract reconstruction for cases with persistent reflux, significant trigonal distortion, or bilateral involvement.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Reflux resolution in approximately 90-95%; febrile UTI elimination in >90% of long-term series

alternativeTreatments.name: Watchful waiting with prophylactic antibiotics
alternativeTreatments.description: In asymptomatic neonates with prenatally detected minimal dilatation and preserved upper pole function, some centres adopt a period of antibiotic prophylaxis and close imaging follow-up before committing to surgery, particularly when the ureterocele is small and the clinical picture is stable.

alternativeTreatments.name: Percutaneous nephrostomy
alternativeTreatments.description: Rarely used as a bridging measure in critically ill neonates with severe obstruction and urosepsis who are not stable enough for definitive surgery; allows drainage of the obstructed system and control of infection before elective repair.

whySeeSpecialist: Cecoureterocele management requires a paediatric urologist experienced in endoscopic and reconstructive techniques, supported by a paediatric nephrologist for long-term renal function monitoring. Specialist input is essential from the time of diagnosis because decisions about the timing and type of surgery directly affect how much functional renal parenchyma is preserved. Centres performing more than 15-20 ureterocele repairs a year have lower complication rates and higher rates of single-stage success.
doctorSelectionGuide: Seek a paediatric urologist at a centre with dedicated paediatric urology and nephrology services, access to nuclear renal scintigraphy and paediatric MR urography, and a track record in endoscopic ureterocele incision and laparoscopic heminephroureterectomy. Ask how many ureterocele cases the surgeon handles per year and what proportion require a second operation. For long-term follow-up, paediatric nephrology involvement helps monitor blood pressure and renal function through childhood and into adolescence.
preventionStrategies: Prenatal ultrasound screening at 18-20 weeks identifies upper pole hydronephrosis, enabling postnatal planning before symptoms develop.
Genetic counselling for families with a previously affected child may prompt early renal ultrasound in subsequent siblings.
Prompt treatment of febrile UTIs in infants reduces the risk of upper tract scarring before the underlying anomaly is identified.
Antibiotic prophylaxis from the time of diagnosis until surgical repair minimises infection-related renal damage.
lifestyleModifications: Administer antibiotic prophylaxis exactly as prescribed and do not stop without specialist guidance.
Ensure adequate oral hydration to maintain urine flow and reduce infection risk.
Report any fever in the child to the treating team promptly — even a low-grade fever in an infant with a known urinary anomaly needs urine testing.
Attend all follow-up imaging appointments; silent ongoing obstruction or new reflux can occur without symptoms.
Monitor blood pressure at every paediatric visit once the diagnosis is established.

recommended: Age-appropriate fluid intake to ensure well-diluted urine
A balanced diet supporting normal childhood growth, which is sometimes impaired by chronic renal disease
Low-sodium diet if hypertension develops due to renal scarring
Adequate calcium and phosphate intake for children on long-term low-sodium or renal-protective regimens
avoid: Excessive salt intake if blood pressure is elevated or renal function is reduced
High-protein diets in children with significant functional renal impairment
Drinks with high oxalate content if stones have complicated the clinical picture

exerciseGuidelines: There is no restriction on physical activity in children following successful surgical correction with preserved renal function. Children with a single functioning kidney (after nephroureterectomy) are generally advised to avoid high-contact collision sports to protect the remaining kidney, though this is a shared decision with the family and specialist. Normal schooling and activity are encouraged.
dailyManagement: Give antibiotic prophylaxis consistently — missed doses significantly raise UTI risk.
Check urine if the child is feverish or has changed voiding behaviour.
Keep a simple symptom diary noting voiding frequency, stream quality, and any febrile episodes for review at clinic appointments.
Ensure blood pressure is checked at every visit to paediatrics or general practice.
Plan imaging follow-up as directed — typically renal ultrasound 4-6 weeks post-operatively and again at 3-6 months.
prognosis: When identified early and managed in a paediatric urology centre, the prognosis for children with cecoureterocele is generally good. Preserved renal function at diagnosis is the strongest predictor of long-term outcome. Upper pole moieties with less than 10% differential function are typically non-salvageable and are removed, but the remaining functioning kidney tissue is adequate for normal childhood and adult life in the great majority of cases. Studies of duplex ureterocele managed with heminephroureterectomy report normal or near-normal total renal function in 85-90% of children at follow-up extending to 10 years. The rate of subsequent bladder surgery after initial upper tract surgery is approximately 30-40% in some series, reflecting the complexity of achieving reflux resolution. Children who sustain significant upper tract scarring from repeated febrile UTIs before diagnosis carry a higher risk of hypertension and chronic kidney disease in adulthood, underlining the importance of prompt diagnosis.
recoveryTimeline: Endoscopic incision is a day-case or overnight procedure, with return to normal activity within a few days. Laparoscopic heminephroureterectomy typically requires a 2-3 day inpatient stay with return to normal activity in 2-4 weeks. Open lower tract reconstruction involves 3-5 inpatient days and 4-6 weeks' recovery. Imaging follow-up continues for months to years to confirm resolution of hydronephrosis and reflux.
complications: Persistent or new-onset vesicoureteral reflux after endoscopic incision — occurs in 30-50% and may require further surgery.
Recurrent febrile UTIs leading to progressive upper pole scarring and loss of renal parenchyma.
Ureterocele prolapse causing acute urethral obstruction — a urological emergency requiring prompt reduction and urgent repair.
Contralateral ureter reflux caused by trigonal distortion from a large ureterocele, resulting in bilateral upper tract risk.
Chronic kidney disease or hypertension in adulthood following significant early-life upper tract damage.
Post-operative ureteric stricture or anastomotic obstruction requiring revision surgery.

supportResources.name: Society for Pediatric Urology — Patient Education
supportResources.description: Patient and family resources from the leading North American paediatric urology professional organisation.

supportResources.name: NIDDK — Hydronephrosis in Children
supportResources.description: Plain-English overview from the National Institute of Diabetes and Digestive and Kidney Diseases on hydronephrosis management in children.

supportResources.name: American Urological Association Foundation
supportResources.description: Patient education hub with articles on congenital urinary tract anomalies in children.

sources.title: Coplen DE, Austin JC. Outcome analysis of prenatal and postnatal diagnosis of dilating ureteroceles. J Urol 2004;172:1640-2
Bolduc S, Upadhyay J, Reidy M, et al. Ureteroceles: 26-year experience. J Urol 2003;170:1913-7

confusedWithConditions.name: Orthotopic ureterocele
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: An orthotopic ureterocele sits entirely within the bladder trigone and does not prolapse into the urethra, whereas a cecoureterocele's body extends below the bladder neck, risking urethral obstruction.

confusedWithConditions.name: Vesicoureteral reflux
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Primary VUR causes urine to flow backward from bladder to kidney without an obstructing intravesical cyst; cecoureterocele can cause secondary VUR but presents primarily with an obstructing pouch on imaging.

confusedWithConditions.name: Bladder exstrophy
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Bladder exstrophy involves complete anterior wall absence with the bladder plate exposed externally, a dramatic structural difference from the internal intravesical cyst of cecoureterocele.

relatedConditions.name: Orthotopic ureterocele
relatedConditions.relevance: The most closely related structural variant; the two subtypes share the same developmental mechanism and are managed within the same surgical paradigm.

relatedConditions.name: Duplication of ureter
relatedConditions.relevance: The underlying anatomical substrate for most cecoureteroceles — the upper pole ureter of a duplex system is the site of the anomaly in the majority of cases.

relatedConditions.name: Vesicoureteral reflux
relatedConditions.relevance: Trigonal distortion from the cecoureterocele causes reflux into the ipsilateral lower pole or contralateral system in over half of affected children, directly influencing management.

relatedConditions.name: Posterior urethral valves
relatedConditions.relevance: Another congenital obstructive uropathy presenting in male neonates with voiding difficulty and hydronephrosis, an important differential when the sex and imaging pattern differ from typical cecoureterocele.

faqs.question: What is a cecoureterocele?
faqs.answer: A cecoureterocele is a congenital cystic pouch at the lower end of the ureter that balloons into the bladder and extends into the urethra. It can block urine flow and cause infections. Most cases are found in girls with a duplicated kidney system.

faqs.question: How is a cecoureterocele different from a regular ureterocele?
faqs.answer: A standard orthotopic ureterocele sits fully inside the bladder. A cecoureterocele's body extends under the bladder lining into the urethra, which means it can intermittently block urinary outflow and may prolapse out of the urethra entirely — neither feature occurs with the orthotopic type.

faqs.question: How common is cecoureterocele?
faqs.answer: Ureteroceles overall affect roughly 1 in 4,000 children, and cecoureteroceles make up about 5-10% of that group, making the condition genuinely rare. Most are identified in female infants during workup for hydronephrosis found on prenatal ultrasound or after a first febrile urinary tract infection.

faqs.question: What causes a cecoureterocele?
faqs.answer: It develops when the terminal portion of the ureter fails to incorporate normally into the bladder wall during weeks 5-8 of fetal development. The resulting weak, obstructed segment balloons under urine pressure. Most cases arise in the upper pole ureter of a duplicated collecting system, and some families carry a genetic predisposition.

faqs.question: How is cecoureterocele diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis begins with a renal and bladder ultrasound, which may show a cystic pouch at the bladder base and an enlarged upper pole. A voiding cystourethrogram (VCUG) delineates the full pouch extent and detects reflux. Nuclear renal scintigraphy (DMSA or MAG3) measures how much function the affected upper pole retains. Cystoscopy under anaesthesia confirms the anatomy before surgery.

faqs.question: Does a cecoureterocele always need surgery?
faqs.answer: Surgery is almost always required to relieve obstruction and protect renal function. The simplest approach is endoscopic incision (a small cut made through a cystoscope), which is curative in some cases. When the upper pole kidney is poorly functioning, removal of that segment (heminephroureterectomy) is the standard approach. Watchful waiting with antibiotics may be used briefly while planning surgery in stable infants.

faqs.question: What is endoscopic ureterocele incision?
faqs.answer: Endoscopic incision is a cystoscopic procedure in which a laser or electrocautery instrument makes a small opening at the base of the ureterocele to relieve obstruction. It is the least invasive option, usually done as a day case, and resolves obstruction in over 90% of cases — though many children still need further surgery later, particularly for reflux.

faqs.question: What is heminephroureterectomy?
faqs.answer: Heminephroureterectomy removes the dysplastic upper pole portion of a duplicated kidney along with its ureter. It is the standard operation when the upper pole provides less than 10-15% of total kidney function — the situation in most duplex cecoureteroceles. It can be performed laparoscopically in most children and cures the obstruction in essentially all cases.

faqs.question: Will my child need more than one operation?
faqs.answer: Many children with cecoureterocele — particularly those with a duplex system — require staged surgery. An initial endoscopic incision may relieve the obstruction but leave reflux that needs further repair. Overall, about 30-50% of children treated with initial endoscopic puncture require a second procedure. Your surgeon should outline the likely pathway at the first consultation.

faqs.question: Can a cecoureterocele affect the other kidney?
faqs.answer: Yes. A large cecoureterocele distorts the bladder trigone, and in roughly 20-30% of cases this causes vesicoureteral reflux into the opposite, structurally normal ureter. Imaging of both upper tracts — including a voiding cystourethrogram — is therefore performed at diagnosis to assess both sides.

faqs.question: What is the long-term outlook for kidney function?
faqs.answer: Children whose cecoureterocele is identified early and treated before significant scarring occurs generally retain normal or near-normal overall renal function. Studies show 85-90% of children maintain adequate total kidney function at 10-year follow-up. Those with substantial upper tract scarring from repeated infections before diagnosis carry a higher risk of high blood pressure and chronic kidney disease in adulthood.

faqs.question: Is antibiotic prophylaxis necessary?
faqs.answer: Yes, prophylactic antibiotics — usually low-dose trimethoprim or cephalexin given once daily — are started at diagnosis and continued until surgical correction. Without prophylaxis, the obstructed, stagnant urine above the ureterocele is a fertile environment for bacteria, and febrile infections can scar the functional kidney tissue.

faqs.question: Can cecoureterocele be detected before birth?
faqs.answer: Many cases are first suspected on the routine 20-week anomaly scan or a later prenatal ultrasound that shows one-sided upper renal pole dilatation. However, the diagnosis of cecoureterocele specifically — versus other causes of hydronephrosis — is usually confirmed postnatally with renal ultrasound and VCUG once the infant is stable.

faqs.question: What happens if a cecoureterocele prolipses through the urethra?
faqs.answer: Prolapse of the ureterocele out of the urethral meatus is a rare but urgent event — the child is unable to void and is in pain. It appears as a smooth, glistening pink-red mass at the opening of the urethra in a girl. Immediate urological attention is needed: the prolapse is reduced and emergency catheterisation may be required before definitive surgery is arranged.

faqs.question: Does cecoureterocele run in families?
faqs.answer: There is a familial tendency. Duplex kidneys — the underlying anatomical finding in most cecoureteroceles — have an estimated 5-12% recurrence risk among first-degree relatives. Families with one affected child are often offered a screening renal ultrasound for siblings, though no universal screening guideline currently exists.

faqs.question: How often is follow-up needed after surgery?
faqs.answer: Imaging follow-up is typically conducted at 4-6 weeks post-operatively (renal ultrasound), then at 3-6 months, and annually thereafter until the urinary tract is confirmed stable and reflux has resolved. Blood pressure monitoring continues through childhood. Most children are discharged from urology follow-up by mid-childhood if investigations are consistently normal.

faqs.question: What is the risk of high blood pressure after cecoureterocele?
faqs.answer: Children who develop significant renal scarring — from repeated febrile infections or chronic upper pole obstruction before treatment — carry an elevated lifetime risk of hypertension driven by the renin-angiotensin system. Regular blood pressure measurement at every health visit is recommended throughout childhood and into adulthood for this reason.

faqs.question: Can adults be diagnosed with cecoureterocele?
faqs.answer: Occasionally adults are diagnosed with a ureterocele incidentally found on ultrasound or CT performed for another reason. In adults with minimal symptoms and preserved kidney function, conservative management with close imaging surveillance may be appropriate. Symptomatic cases — recurring infections, obstruction, or reflux — are managed surgically on the same principles as in children.

faqs.question: Is cecoureterocele the same as an ectopic ureterocele?
faqs.answer: No. An ectopic ureterocele has its ureteral orifice opening below the bladder neck — into the urethra, vestibule, or vagina — causing continuous dribbling incontinence. A cecoureterocele has its orifice inside the bladder but its body extending under the trigone into the urethra. The distinction guides surgical planning, particularly the likelihood of causing reflux after endoscopic treatment.

faqs.question: What should I watch for after my child's ureterocele surgery?
faqs.answer: After surgery, report any fever above 38°C promptly, as it may indicate a urinary infection requiring treatment. Watch for changes in voiding pattern — new straining, dribbling, or reduced stream. Return to clinic on schedule for ultrasound checks. Blood in the urine in the first week post-operatively is normal after endoscopic incision but should be reported if heavy or persistent beyond 5-7 days.

metaDescription: Cecoureterocele is a rare congenital ureter cyst extending into the urethra, causing UTIs and voiding difficulty in children. Learn symptoms, diagnosis, and surgery options.
